project_name: MIDI Harmony Analyzer & Melody-Aware Voicing Generator Rule DB
purpose: Rule database and analysis philosophy for a piano accompaniment harmony analyzer that evaluates MIDI against jazz/pop accompaniment rules while remaining sensitive to arpeggio context, melody support, inversion logic, playability, and voice leading. Extended with enharmonic handling, generalized secondary-dominant logic, style-aware voicing decisions, and melody-constrained voicing selection. Further refined with bass-priority root selection and structural promotion of sustained/accented 7th/9th tones Added humanized-timing tolerance for non-quantized performance MIDI so slight anticipations, overlaps, and delayed releases near beat/bar boundaries are not over-read as immediate harmonic changes Also distinguishes real note-length occupancy from pedal-like overlap, treats melodic/arpeggiated bass motion cautiously, and enforces bass-aware final labeling so upper-structure sonorities never erase the actual sounding bass. Further refined with contextual incomplete-voicing handling so shell voicings, omitted tones, and partial right-hand fragments are not falsely rejected; added observed-pitch-set integrity checks so functional labels cannot ignore clearly sounding tones; and formalized separation between literal pitch-set / upper-structure detection / final bass-aware functional labeling. Extended with meter-verification logic that cross-checks MIDI metadata time signatures against actual onset-pattern grouping, bass accent cycles, velocity profiles, and subdivision regularity, so that incorrectly exported meters (e.g. 6/8 exported as 4/4) are detected and corrected before harmonic segmentation begins. Strengthened with melody-prioritized voicing-generation policy so accompaniment voicings are selected from chord-plus-melody context rather than naive close-position chord-tone stacking, with explicit penalties for repeated closed root stacks, melody collisions, and unsupported duplication of melody-declared function tones. Integrated with a dedicated generation engine that treats bass-line design, inversion comparison, guide-tone locking, and candidate-based voice-leading as first-class compositional steps rather than post-hoc evaluation only. Further refined with conservative non-chord-bass slash labeling and hard sus4-first blocking when the 4th is present above bass and the 3rd is absent. Added explicit anti-overpromotion safeguards so same-root family recoverability alone can never promote a brief 7th/9th/11th/13th into the final structural label; each extension must independently qualify through duration, metric strength, repetition, or re-articulation, otherwise it remains surface-only. Added anti-inertia enforcement so the analyzer cannot keep an initial chord hypothesis by momentum after a meaningful mid-bar bass/shell change; new-root events now force split-audit, bass-first reanalysis, and explicit rejection reasons whenever a possible within-bar harmony change is not segmented. Added execution guardrails that force a pre-output verification pass so bass carry-over, slash necessity, and provisional candidate states cannot be prematurely simplified into cleaner root-position labels before final emission. Finalized with db_storage_schema so analyzed harmonic events, reusable pattern fragments, and retrieval tags can be stored in a generation-ready format for later melody-aware retrieval and voicing reuse. Further extended with song-form-aware section analysis that infers likely structural sections through multi-cue scoring, stores confidence and secondary tags, and allows unknown/ambiguous section labels instead of forcing fixed bar-count form assumptions. Further refined with strict separation between harmony-analysis mode and generation/voicing-validation mode, so melody notes cannot back-fill missing accompaniment chord tones during primary harmonic labeling. Also strengthened with slash-over-omitted-3rd-tension preference when a clear upper triad or shell is present over a different bass, and with quality-undecided safeguards so no-3rd sonorities must test sus4/slash logic before defaulting to minor7 or half-diminished families.

summary: Do not label harmony only by collecting all sounding pitches equally.
Separate structural harmony from surface notes.

Strictly separate harmony-analysis mode from generation/voicing-validation mode. Analysis decides what the accompaniment actually plays; generation decides whether a melody-aware voicing is acceptable.

In harmony-analysis mode, accompaniment chord labels must be derived from accompaniment and bass evidence only. Melody notes may be stored as separate role/context information, but must not supply missing 3rds, 7ths, or tensions to the accompaniment label.
In arpeggio/accompaniment contexts, short decorative notes should not automatically force richer chord labels.
Prefer stable, musically practical chord labels over overly literal pitch-set labels.
Do not let an initially selected chord label lock the segment. A meaningful new bass-supported shell inside the bar must trigger fresh local analysis rather than inertia-preserving carryover.
Return a primary label plus alternate candidates with confidence and reasons.
Melody has higher priority than accompaniment top-note freedom.
Reject or penalize voicings that are physically impractical or aesthetically muddy on piano.
Treat rules as weighted preferences, not absolute truth, except for hard constraints.

Because MIDI stores pitch classes without true enharmonic spelling, do not over-penalize or mislabel harmony solely due to spelling ambiguity such as E# vs F or Cb vs B; resolve naming by harmonic context.
When a dominant-resolution path is strongly supported, a single ambiguous chromatic note should not automatically overturn an otherwise well-supported structural chord.

Do not assume every non-diatonic chord is a secondary dominant; evaluate borrowed color, modal interchange, chromatic planing, suspensions, passing diminished, pedal harmony, and tritone-sub behavior before forcing a dominant-function label.
Roman-numeral understanding should be derived from estimated key center plus local tonicization, then exposed separately from the literal chord label.
When the same pitch collection supports multiple labels, prioritize the label whose root is directly supported by the actual bass note before choosing a rootless or alternate-root interpretation.

When the sounding bass and observed tones already form a clear same-root shell such as m7, maj7, or dominant7, prefer that direct same-root family before promoting an alternate upper-structure-derived label.
If the 7th or 9th is metrically strong and sustained long enough to sound structural, promote it from surface color to the structural chord label rather than collapsing everything to a plain triad.

Same-root family recoverability alone is never sufficient to promote a 7th, 9th, 11th, or 13th into the final structural label. If the extension itself is brief, off-beat, decorative, or terminal, keep the plain triad/shell as primary and store the extension only as surface color.
Do not output a root label that contradicts the sounding bass without explicitly marking the inversion/slash relationship.
When a coherent upper triad or shell is clearly heard over a different bass, prefer the literal slash-chord reading before rewriting the sonority as a same-root omitted-3rd tension label.
In non-quantized performance MIDI, slight anticipations, overlaps, and delayed releases near beat or bar boundaries should be treated cautiously before declaring a structural chord change.

Distinguish expressive performance timing from structural harmonic timing; a newly appearing note near a boundary must earn structural status through beat strength, duration, repetition, bass support, or confirmation in the next segment.
When note length itself is explicitly encoded in performance MIDI, long-duration notes and sustained bass occupancy should be treated as stronger structural evidence than brief arrivals.

Do not mistake arpeggio traversal, melodic passing motion, or bass-line movement for an automatic harmonic relabel unless the new root also gains structural duration, supporting tones, and metrical confirmation.

Upper-structure recognition and final chord labeling are separate stages: even if the upper notes resemble a diminished or tension-heavy sonority, the selected label must remain bass-aware and must not erase the actual sounding bass.

Do not treat incomplete voicings as automatic analysis failure; recover likely function from bass, surrounding context, and resolution behavior when the observed voicing is a realistic shell or omission pattern.

However, do not invent absent tones carelessly when the actually sounding pitch-set already strongly points to another local sonority; missing-tone recovery is contextual support, not a license to ignore real notes.
Keep three views distinct when useful: literal sounding set, upper-structure/partial-voicing interpretation, and final bass-aware selected label.
Do not let bass-melody protection become so strong that it erases a genuine within-bar harmonic split supported by new bass duration and upper-structure reorganization.

Do not blindly trust the MIDI file's time-signature metadata. Many DAWs export 6/8, 3/4, 5/4, or other meters as 4/4 by default. Before committing to the metadata time signature, verify it against actual note-onset grouping patterns, bass accent cycles, and rhythmic periodicity in the performed MIDI data.
When the detected onset-grouping pattern conflicts with the MIDI metadata time signature, prefer the pattern-derived meter for harmonic segmentation and report the discrepancy explicitly.
When generating accompaniment MIDI, do not map chord labels directly to default close-position stacks; melody-plus-context must guide actual voicing choice.
If the melody already states a structural function tone such as 3rd or 7th, accompaniment need not redundantly duplicate that tone unless texture, emphasis, or style specifically benefits from it.
Different melodies over the same chord may require different voicings; voicing selection is a contextual decision, not a fixed template lookup.
For generation mode, choose bass contour before fully realizing inner voices; a good bass line is not a byproduct but a design decision.
Pattern-aware inversion logic should inform candidate ranking, but melody protection and playability remain higher-priority overrides.
Guide-tone continuity is one of the most reliable ways to prevent MIDI accompaniment from sounding mechanically chord-stacked.

Do not confuse bass-priority root selection with a ban on slash labels. When a functional interpretation has strong resolution evidence and the actual bass is an inversion of that function, the correct action is to write the functional label with a slash indicating the real bass — not to abandon the functional interpretation entirely.

Analysis must follow a strict procedural pipeline for every single segment without exception: (1) identify the actual sounding bass, (2) collect all structural pitches, (3) test same-root shell from the bass, (4) test functional/slash interpretations where the bass is an inversion, (5) check forward resolution and backward context, (6) select the label. Skipping any step or reordering is forbidden.

When a sus4 tone (perfect 4th above root) is structurally present and the expected 3rd is absent, prefer a sus4 label over a minor-7th shell built from a different root. Confirm by checking whether the 3rd appears in the next segment as a resolution target.

Pattern-matching a familiar chord shape from the upper structure and then retrofitting the bass is the single most common analysis error. The pipeline must start from the bass upward, never from the upper structure downward.

When an upper structure is clear but the bass is a non-chord tone or an unconfirmed functional extension relative to that upper structure, default to a bare slash label such as D/C rather than automatically absorbing the bass into the chord quality as D7/C. Promote the bass into the chord-quality label only when metric strength, sustain, supporting tones, and contextual resolution explicitly confirm that function.

When the perfect 4th above the actual bass is structurally present and neither the minor nor major 3rd is sounding, lock the analysis into a sus4-first branch. Alternate-root minor-7 interpretations are temporarily blocked until the sus4 reading is explicitly rejected.

If neither the major nor minor 3rd above the actual bass is structurally present, keep chord quality undecided until sus4, slash, shell-context, and forward-resolution checks are completed; do not default early to minor7 or half-diminished families.

If a root-defining bass change, shell-defining tone appearance, or functional reinterpretation occurs from beat 3 or later within a measure, split the measure into separate harmonic segments rather than assigning one label to the whole bar.
A chord label is valid only for the local segment whose bass and structural tones support it; do not retroactively overwrite beat 1-2 with a harmony that becomes valid only later in the bar.
Do not rush from recognizable upper-structure shapes to a neat final label; candidate recognition and final emission are separate acts.
When evidence is split, prefer the less simplified but bass-preserving label over a cleaner root-position label.
A correct but uglier slash label is preferable to a prettier label that erases carried bass reality.

analysis_layers.description: The main chord identity for the segment/bar/beat.
Passing tones, arpeggio traversal notes, decorative extensions, neighbor tones, suspensions, and melody-driven color notes that do not necessarily redefine the structural chord.
examples: E as 9 over Dm7 in an arpeggio
F as b13 over Am7 candidate but not primary label

analysis_layers.description: Phrase context, surrounding chords, bass motion, inversion preference, melody relation, and accompaniment style determine the final preferred label.

hard_constraints.description: Violations here should reject candidates or heavily penalize them.

rules.description: Avoid harsh minor ninth / major seventh clashes between melody and accompaniment top note on strong beats.
Reject voicings that exceed reasonable hand span unless advanced mode is explicitly enabled.
Avoid dense clusters in low register.
Avoid right hand consistently placed below left hand top note unless intentionally marked as special texture.
Non-chord tones that are not explainable as passing, neighbor, suspension, arpeggio color, or melody-support note should be heavily penalized.

Final chord labels must not use a catch-all dominant label in place of explicitly supported dominant tensions. Write supported dominant tensions directly in the final label when structurally justified, and do not promote weak ornamental tones into final tension text.

Do not choose a cleaner or more familiar final label if doing so erases the chord's heard identity. Labels must not simplify away structurally meaningful bass notes, slash/inversion information, dominant-defining tensions, or a locally independent sonority that is musically supported.
Do not choose accompaniment upper voices that create an exposed minor-9 collision against the active melody on a strong beat unless explicitly marked as stylistically intended.
Do not repeatedly emit naive root-3-5-7 close-position stacks across adjacent chords when smoother melody-aware or voice-led options are available.

Every harmonic segment must be analyzed through the full pipeline in strict order: (1) identify actual sounding bass note, (2) collect all structural pitches with duration/metrical weighting, (3) test same-root chord family from the actual bass, (4) test functional slash-chord interpretations where the bass is a chord tone of a different root, (5) check forward resolution and backward context for functional confirmation, (6) select final label. The analyzer must not skip steps, reorder steps, or use upper-structure pattern-matching as a shortcut that bypasses bass identification. Violation of this pipeline order is a hard constraint failure regardless of whether the final label happens to be correct.

An early chord guess inside a bar must remain provisional. If a later beat introduces a durationally meaningful new bass, shell-defining upper-tone reorganization, or a locally interpretable new sonority, the analyzer MUST reopen the segment-local decision instead of preserving the first label by inertia. Carrying the first label forward without explicit re-evaluation is a hard constraint failure even if the final merged label looks musically plausible.

Whenever a new bass arrival occurs on a strong or medium-strong beat, the analyzer must run a split audit before keeping a single-bar label. The audit checks: (1) strong/medium-strong bass change, (2) shell or upper-structure reformation, (3) inability to explain the new sonority as same-root surface color only, (4) at least approximately one beat of durational support or immediate contextual confirmation. If two or more checks pass, preserving one unsplit label is forbidden unless an explicit counter-reason is recorded.

If the analyzer decides NOT to split after a meaningful new-bass event, it must explicitly state why the split was rejected. Valid reasons are limited to cases such as pure passing-bass motion without shell support, anticipation/overlap explained by humanized timing tolerance, or a non-interpretable upper sonority. Keeping the previous label without one of these explicit rejection reasons is a hard failure.

When a new bass event is durationally meaningful enough to challenge the current label, the analyzer must restart the segment-local pipeline from bass identification for the post-change region. It is forbidden to begin from the previously chosen upper-structure interpretation and merely ask whether the old label can survive. Bass-first reanalysis is mandatory.

If a harmony becomes convincing only after beat 2, beat 3, or any later local arrival, that later confirmation is segment-local evidence only. It must not be used to retroactively flatten the entire bar into one representative chord. Any analysis that lets late confirmation overwrite earlier unsupported beats is a hard constraint failure.

A slash chord label such as A7(b9)/C# does NOT violate bass-priority rules when the slash denominator correctly states the actual sounding bass. Bass-priority rules forbid labels whose unslashed root contradicts the bass without acknowledgment. They do not forbid functional root identification when the bass is explicitly notated as an inversion via slash notation. Confusing these two situations — treating slash-label permission as if it were a bass-violation ban — is itself a hard constraint failure.

examples.WRONG_reasoning: A is not sounding in the bass, so A7(b9) cannot be primary label → fall back to C#°7
examples.CORRECT_reasoning: C# is the 3rd of A7 → A7(b9)/C# is a valid bass-aware functional label → forward resolution to D confirms dominant function → select A7(b9)/C#

rules.description: When the actual sounding pitches above a bass note include the perfect 4th but not the minor 3rd (or major 3rd), do not force a minor-7th shell interpretation by choosing a different root. Test the sus4 interpretation first. If the 3rd appears in the immediately following segment as a resolution, the sus4 label is strongly confirmed. This is especially critical for dominant-function contexts where sus4 → 3rd resolution is a standard pattern.

examples.reason: A is the 4th not the 3rd, no 3rd is sounding, next segment resolves 4th to 3rd → textbook sus4 dominant

rules.description: If the upper structure clearly forms a triad or shell of a chord root but the actual sounding bass is a non-chord tone or only an unconfirmed functional extension relative to that root, do NOT automatically absorb the bass into the chord-quality label. The default action is a bare slash label that preserves the literal bass, such as D/C rather than D7/C. Promotion to a functional extension label is allowed only when the bass tone is metrically strong, sustained, supported by surrounding tones, and contextually confirmed by resolution.

examples.promotion_condition: Only promote to D7/C if C is structurally sustained/strong, supported as the 7th by context, and functionally confirmed by the following resolution.

rules.description: When the perfect 4th above the actual bass is structurally present and neither the minor nor major 3rd is sounding, the analyzer MUST enter a sus4-first branch and temporarily block alternate-root minor-7 interpretations. Those alternate-root readings may only be reconsidered after the sus4 interpretation has been explicitly rejected by duration, metric weakness, contradiction from surrounding tones, or failed resolution evidence.

examples.next_segment_reveal: G# appears in the following segment
examples.reason: A is a structural sus4 above E, the 3rd is absent, and the following G# confirms sus4 to dominant-3rd resolution.

rules.description: If a root-defining bass change, shell-defining tone appearance, or functional reinterpretation occurs mid-measure, the measure must be split into separate harmonic segments. Do not assign a later harmony retroactively to the entire measure.

examples.beats_1_2: A-7 supported by A bass and minor-7 shell tones
examples.beats_3_4: A7/C# supported by new C# bass and dominant reinterpretation
examples.reason: The dominant/inversion reading becomes valid only from beat 3 after the bass change, so the bar must be segmented.

rules.description: A selected chord label is valid only for the segment whose observed bass and structural tones support it. A harmony that becomes valid on beat 3 or later must not overwrite the label of beat 1-2 unless the earlier segment independently supports the same interpretation.

examples.forbidden_action: retroactively relabel the whole measure as A7/C#
examples.required_action: keep labels local to each segment and report the within-bar split explicitly

rules.description: Do not promote a same-root triad/shell to maj7, m7, 7, add9, maj9, 11, 13, or tension-bearing variants merely because the family would be musically readable if a brief extension note were accepted. Same-root recoverability may restore omitted root/3rd/5th/7th logic only when the promoted extension itself has independent structural evidence. A newly appearing extension that is brief, off-beat, decorative, terminal, or confined to ornamental arpeggiation must remain in surface_extensions rather than the final structural label.
forbidden_behaviors: Do not output Fmaj7 when F and A are structural but E appears only as a brief terminal decorative note.
Do not output Dadd9 or Dmaj9 when E is merely a passing or ornamental arpeggio tone.
Do not convert a readable same-root family possibility into the final label unless the extension itself earns promotion.

examples.input_context: F and A occupy the segment structurally; E appears only as a final brief 16th-note decoration.
examples.reason: The same-root family is audible, but the 7th itself lacks independent structural qualification.

examples.input_context: D-F#-A are structural and E is only a brief off-beat traversal note.
examples.reason: Readable naming is subordinate to extension-specific structural evidence.

rules.description: If the previously sounding bass is still acoustically active and no new bass attack has occurred, do not relabel the sonority as a fresh unslashed root-position chord merely because a new upper-structure triad or shell appears above it. Preserve the carried bass in the candidate and final label until a true bass reset is observed.

examples.reason: The upper triad may be recognized, but final relabeling is blocked until the bass actually resets.

rules.description: If slash or inversion information remains necessary to preserve the actual sounding bass, that information must survive into the emitted final label. Do not delete slash information at the last step for readability, familiarity, canonical naming, or bar-level neatness.

Recognition of an upper-structure triad, seventh, quartal cluster, or shell creates a candidate only. It does not by itself authorize the final selected label. Final selection authority remains with bass-aware, segment-local verification.

Before emitting the final label for any segment, re-check whether a new bass attack actually occurred. If the answer is no, block any simplified non-slash relabel that would imply a bass reset. This re-check is mandatory even when the upper structure looks obvious.

Do not emit a new slash/inversion label solely because an arpeggio pattern briefly places one chord tone in the bass at a weak or terminal position. If the event is better explained as the tail of the active harmony and lacks re-anchoring support, keep the active harmony as final output.
Inside confirmed arpeggio texture, a lowest-note change is evidence only, not authority. Final split requires an additional structural cue beyond bass motion alone.

In harmony-analysis mode, the primary accompaniment chord label must be derived from accompaniment and bass evidence only. Melody notes may be analyzed separately as melody-role context, but must not supply missing 3rds, 7ths, or tensions to the accompaniment chord label. Melody-assisted tolerance of omitted chord tones is allowed only in generation, voicing-validation, or arrangement-check mode, not in primary harmonic labeling.

In harmony-analysis mode, do not promote an accompaniment chord to 7, maj7, add9, 9, 11, 13, b9, #9, #11, or b13 in the final primary label unless that specific extension tone is directly observed in the accompaniment with independent structural evidence. Melody notes must never be used to justify promotion of a missing accompaniment 7th or tension. Contextual incomplete-voicing recovery may still support dominant-family candidacy, but unobserved 7ths/tensions must remain unpromoted in the final primary accompaniment label.

If the sounding upper structure forms a coherent triad or shell over the actual bass, prefer the literal slash-chord interpretation before rewriting the sonority as a same-root tension chord with omitted 3rd. For example, prefer G/A over Am11(no3) unless strong structural evidence explicitly confirms that the same-root tension identity is more faithful than the slash reading.

If neither the major 3rd nor minor 3rd above the actual bass is structurally present, chord quality remains undecided. Before assigning minor7, minor7b5, diminished-fragment, or other diatonically convenient default families, the analyzer MUST check for a sus4 reading whenever a structural perfect 4th above the bass is present, and must also compare slash/shell interpretations before settling on a quality label. This rule applies to all ambiguous no-3rd sonorities, not only false alternate-root minor7 cases.

soft_constraints.description: Weighted preferences used for scoring among valid candidates.

rules.description: Prefer retaining one or two common tones between adjacent chords when musically appropriate.
Prefer minimal motion in inner voices unless tension build is intended.

Prefer a musically stable, practical structural chord label over an overly literal pitch-set label only when the simplification preserves the actual harmonic identity. Practical readability may remove surface decoration, but it must not erase bass reality, structurally supported tensions, slash-chord information, or an independently interpretable local sonority.
examples: Prefer Dm7 + surface 9 over forcing Dm9 as structural label in a simple arpeggio context
Prefer Fmaj9/A over Am7(b13) when F-centered context is stronger
Do not reduce E7(b9) to E7 when the b9 is structurally salient
Do not collapse C7/Bb into plain C or C7 if the Bb bass is structurally meaningful

rules.description: A practical final label may be simpler than the full surface pitch collection, but it must preserve the harmonic identity that is actually heard. Do not simplify away structurally meaningful bass placement, slash information, dominant-defining tensions, or a convincingly independent local sonority merely to produce a cleaner label.

Surface decoration may be omitted from the final label when it is brief, ornamental, or non-structural. Harmonic-identity cues must not be omitted. Treat decorative passing tones, ornamental anticipations, and fleeting arpeggio traversal notes as removable surface detail, but preserve actual bass support, structurally supported tensions, chord quality, inversion/slash information, and independently interpretable local harmonic changes.

Prefer structural labels whose root is directly supported by the sounding bass note on strong beats. If an alternate-root interpretation is still useful, present it as a slash/inversion candidate rather than the main unslashed label.
Reward chord labels that explicitly include a 7th or 9th when that extension is metrically strong and durationally salient enough to sound structural rather than merely decorative.

Reward keeping the plain triad or same-root shell as the primary label when a candidate extension is present only briefly, off the beat, as ornamental arpeggiation, or as a terminal decorative note. Do not treat richer naming as automatically superior when the extension itself lacks structural anchoring.

In non-quantized performance MIDI, prefer explanations that treat slight anticipations, overlaps, or delayed releases near beat/bar boundaries as expressive timing unless a new harmony gains sufficient structural support.
Prefer labels supported by notes that occupy meaningful duration in the segment, especially when the bass and chord skeleton remain present longer than brief decorative or anticipatory notes.
Prefer analyses that treat brief scalar, chromatic, or arpeggiated bass movement as melodic bass motion before promoting it to a new chord root.

Treat incomplete voicings, shell voicings, omitted 5ths, and partial right-hand fragments as valid real-world evidence. Do not hard-reject a candidate only because root/3rd/7th are not all present if bass support, surrounding harmony, and resolution behavior strongly recover the function.

Reward candidates that remain faithful to clearly sounding structural tones. Functional reinterpretation may fill in likely omitted tones, but it must not casually ignore strong, sustained, or metrically important observed tones that point to another local sonority.

When upper notes suggest an independent sonority such as a diminished seventh or tension-heavy structure, analyze that upper structure first, then convert to a final label that still reflects the actual bass and broader context.

If the melody clearly states an important structural tone such as the 3rd, 7th, or a sanctioned structural tension, reduce priority for thick internal duplication of that same tone unless style, dynamics, or register balance justify it.
Prefer inversions that improve the combined smoothness of bass, inner voices, and melody interaction rather than judging inversion by bass alone.
Apply penalty when the engine falls back to default closed root-position stacks without checking melody support, common-tone retention, and top-voice motion.

When two candidate labels compete — one being a literal root-position reading and the other being a functional slash-chord interpretation — and the forward/backward resolution strongly confirms the functional reading, prefer the functional slash label as primary. The literal root-position reading becomes the alternate. Resolution evidence includes: dominant-to-tonic motion, secondary dominant chains, and standard cadential patterns.

examples.reason: V7(b9)/V → V resolution is textbook; C#°7 root-position label obscures this function

rules.description: If during analysis the engine recognizes a familiar upper-structure shape (e.g., diminished 7th, major triad) before identifying the bass, apply a self-check penalty and restart from bass identification. This penalty exists because upper-structure-first pattern matching is the primary cause of bass-contradicting labels and false slash notation.

In repeating arpeggio texture, prefer retaining the currently active harmony over introducing a new local label when the new low note is explainable as a chord tone within the same harmony and no new re-anchoring cues appear.
Raise the split threshold in arpeggio texture relative to block-chord or clearly re-attacked accompaniment. Reward consistency when a whole broken-chord cell supports one harmony.

arpeggio_sensitive_analysis.description: Short decorative notes in an arpeggio should not automatically redefine structural harmony.

rules.description: Weight bass notes, long notes, metrically strong notes, and repeated tones more heavily than short traversal tones.
If a color tone appears briefly in arpeggio context but the structural triad/seventh remains stable, tag it as surface extension instead of forcing a structural relabel.

examples.input_pitch_set_context: D-F-A with brief E in arpeggio

rules.description: Do not continuously relabel every decorative arpeggio tone as a new chord unless accent, duration, repetition, or bass support justifies it.

When the previous harmony clearly functions as a dominant toward the current target, brief non-structural chromatic notes in the target chord should be treated as surface decoration before considering a structural relabel.

The arpeggio simplification rule applies to brief traversal notes only. If a 7th or other extension is sustained on a strong beat, do not demote that specific extension to surface color automatically. Evaluate each extension independently for structural promotion.

In humanized accompaniment MIDI, arpeggiated upper notes, melodic passing notes, and brief bass traversal notes should first be interpreted as motion within the current harmony. Do not promote them to a new chord unless they also gain structural duration, supporting chord tones, and metric confirmation.

Before applying normal bass-first split logic, test whether the current region behaves like a repeating broken-chord / arpeggio texture. Repeating arpeggio cells, sequential unfolding of chord tones, and stable accompaniment patterns should activate arpeggio-aware parsing mode.

In arpeggio-aware mode, distinguish sounding bass from harmonic bass. The lowest struck note is not automatically the harmonic anchor. Keep the harmonic bass on the active harmony unless the newly appearing low note proves a true local re-anchoring.

In arpeggio-aware mode, a newly appearing lowest note at a weak position, phrase tail, or end-of-pattern location must not be promoted to an independent slash chord if it can be explained as a chord tone of the current active harmony and does not establish a new harmonic center into the next attack window.

In arpeggio-aware mode, bass change alone is insufficient for split. Require at least one additional re-anchoring cue such as shell reformation, guide-tone confirmation, strong-beat onset, repeated reinforcement, or continuation into the next pattern unit.

If the newly low note, upper notes, and rhythmic placement are better explained as redistribution of the currently active harmony rather than a genuinely new sonority, retain the current harmony as final output even if a local slash-chord candidate was temporarily generated.

humanized_timing_tolerance.description: Rules for separating expressive microtiming in human-played MIDI from true harmonic change points.

rules.description: In non-quantized performance MIDI, a note that enters slightly before or after a beat/bar boundary should not by itself trigger an immediate structural chord relabel.

Promote a boundary-adjacent note or note-group to a new structural harmony only if the new sonority gains independent support through beat strength, duration, repetition, bass confirmation, or re-articulation in the following slice.

A newly appearing bass note near the end of a segment should not automatically replace the current structural bass unless it occupies enough of the segment or is confirmed at the next metrically meaningful point.

If the existing chord skeleton remains dominant in duration and metrical weight while one or two notes from the next chord appear early, prefer the existing structural label and treat the incoming notes as overlap/anticipation.

examples.input_context: A-C-Eb occupies most of the bar, B enters only at the very end in humanized timing
examples.surface_or_transition_tag: B as anticipation of next harmony
examples.reason: Late boundary-adjacent B does not independently earn structural control of the current bar.

examples.input_context: Current chord remains present, next-chord note arrives slightly early before the barline
examples.preferred_action: Do not relabel immediately; wait for next-slice confirmation unless bass and multiple structural tones also shift.

rules.description: In human-played accompaniment MIDI, do not assume one bar equals one harmony. If a new bass arrival is accompanied by a meaningful reorganization of the upper structure, allow a true mid-bar chord split even when note endings overlap or the timing is slightly early/late.

When one part of the bar is still colored by the previous chord but a later portion establishes a second clear harmonic center, prefer representing two local chord events rather than forcing the entire bar into one merged label. Residual sustain from the earlier chord should be treated as overlap if the later sonority becomes independently convincing.

examples.input_context: Early in the bar: E bass with G#-D-F-B. Later in the same bar: Bb bass with C-E-G, followed by Am11 in the next arrival.
examples.preferred_analysis: E7(b9) -> C7/Bb within the bar, then Am11
examples.reason: The later Bb-based sonority is not just bass melody; it forms an independently audible second harmonic center before the next chord.

rules.description: When performance MIDI uses actual note lengths instead of pedal automation, privilege duration occupancy. A note or bass movement that is brief relative to the ongoing sonority should normally remain a surface, overlap, or melodic-motion event unless it captures substantial time and structural support.

candidate_labeling_policy.description: Return a preferred label plus alternate candidates with score and explanation.

rules.description: Every ambiguous segment should provide at least one selected label and up to three alternate candidates when relevant.
Bias candidate ranking by phrase context, surrounding harmony, bass placement, chord completeness, and practical readability.
If a candidate strongly explains the surrounding dominant-to-target resolution, boost it above alternatives that only match the raw pitch-set literally.
If a chosen interpretation depends on a different bass than the sounding bass, output the inversion/slash explicitly. Never output an unslashed root label that contradicts the actual bass support.

Do not prefer a functionally elegant analysis over a bass-supported local sonority unless the evidence for the functional reinterpretation is clearly stronger and explicitly documented as an alternate.

If the sounding bass plus observed structural tones already form a convincing same-root shell such as 1-b3-5-b7, 1-3-5-7, or 1-3-5-b7, prefer that same-root chord family as the primary candidate before promoting an alternate upper-structure-derived reading. Treat other apparent upper-structure chord shapes as alternate explanations unless they preserve the heard identity better than the same-root reading.

examples.input_context: Bass Bb with observed Db-F-Ab and additional color tone G
examples.alternate_interpretation: upper-structure-derived reading may be stored as alternate only
examples.reason: The same-root minor-7 shell is already clearly established before considering alternate upper-structure names.

rules.description: Do not relabel a segment solely because a few notes from the next harmony enter slightly early or a few notes from the current harmony release slightly late in non-quantized MIDI. Require structural confirmation before declaring a new primary label.

If the selected harmonic interpretation relies on an upper-structure root that is not the sounding bass, do not output the bare unslashed label. Emit a bass-aware slash form or a functionally reinterpreted bass-aware label instead.

Before promoting a newly heard bass pitch to a new chord root, test whether it is better explained as melodic bass motion, passing bass, line cliché, or arpeggiated traversal inside the current harmony.

Do not collapse dominant sonorities into a generic catch-all label. When a dominant sonority is clearly supported, write the actually supported tensions explicitly, such as 7(b9), 7(#9), 7(b13), 7(#11), or supported combinations like 7(b9,b13). Include only tensions that are structurally supported and musically meaningful in the sonority.

Prefer readable final labels, but never at the cost of harmonic identity. Surface simplification is allowed only when the omitted material is genuinely decorative; if the bass, inversion, chord quality, or structurally supported tensions materially change what the sonority is, keep that information in the selected label.

Do not forbid a functional label solely because one or more core tones are omitted in the local slice. First test whether the observed voicing is a plausible shell/incomplete voicing supported by bass, neighboring segments, and expected resolution.
Missing core tones create uncertainty and should reduce confidence, not trigger an automatic ban. Escalate to alternate-candidate status only when contextual recovery is weak.

Contextual recovery of omitted tones is allowed, but do not choose a final label that conflicts with clearly sounding structural tones unless the conflict is explicitly documented and strongly justified as an alternate interpretation.
If needed, keep literal pitch-set, upper-structure label, and final functional label separate instead of collapsing everything into one forced name.

In harmony-analysis mode, treat melody as separate contextual information. Melody may explain phrase context or later generation decisions, but it must not back-fill missing accompaniment chord tones when ranking primary accompaniment labels.

When a complete or near-complete upper triad/shell is heard over a different bass, rank the literal slash-chord candidate above same-root omitted-3rd tension spellings. Same-root no3 tension labels should only outrank the slash reading when contextual function, duration, and surrounding evidence clearly make the same-root identity more faithful.

When the 3rd is absent, do not default to m7, m7b5, maj7, or dominant-family naming merely because one interpretation looks diatonically convenient. Keep the sonority quality-undecided until sus4, slash, shell-context, and forward-resolution checks are completed.

root_and_extension_resolution.description: Rules for deciding between competing root interpretations and for promoting sustained chord tones into the structural label.

rules.description: When the same pitch collection supports multiple chord names, prefer the label whose root is explicitly supported by the sounding bass note, especially on strong beats. Do not choose an alternate-root label unless the inversion/slash form is written explicitly.

Before choosing a final label derived from an apparent upper-structure sonority, first test whether the sounding bass and observed structural tones already support a direct same-root chord family. This check is especially important for m7, maj7, and dominant-7 shells. If a same-root shell is convincingly present, use that family as the primary label and treat the upper-structure-derived reading as alternate unless it more faithfully represents the heard harmonic identity.
preferred_workflow: test same-root shell completion or strong contextual recovery
add structurally supported tensions/colors to that same-root family
only then compare alternate upper-structure-derived labels

examples.input_context: Bass Bb with Db-F-Ab clearly present and G also sounding
examples.preferred_primary_label: Bbm7 with added color/tension as justified
examples.alternate_label: G-based upper-structure reading stored only as alternate
examples.reason: The same-root Bbm7 shell is already established by the bass and structural tones.

rules.description: Evaluate each extension tone independently. Promote a 7th, 9th, 11th, or 13th from surface color to the structural label only if that specific extension itself is metrically strong, durationally salient, repeated, or otherwise structurally anchored.

promotion_conditions.interpretation: Do not promote all available extensions as a bundle. A strong/sustained 7th can justify maj7 or 7 without automatically justifying 9, 11, or 13. A 9th, 11th, or 13th must separately earn promotion through its own metrical or durational salience.
promotion_conditions.downgrade_rule: If an extension appears only briefly off the beat, as a passing tone, or as an ornamental arpeggio step, keep it in surface_extensions instead of the primary structural label.

examples.reason: maj7 has strong local evidence; no independent evidence yet for 9.

examples.input_pitch_set_context: D-F#-A with strong sustained C# and also strong sustained E
examples.reason: Both maj7 and 9 independently meet promotion criteria.

examples.input_pitch_set_context: D-F#-A with strong sustained E but no structural C#
examples.reason: 9 is promoted independently; maj7 is not inferred when C# lacks structural evidence.

examples.input_pitch_set_context: D-F#-A with strong C# on beat 1 and only a brief off-beat E later
examples.reason: maj7 is structural; 9 remains surface color because E does not independently qualify.

examples.input_pitch_set_context: D-F#-A with only a brief passing E

rules.description: Same-root family recoverability and extension promotion are separate decisions. After identifying a plausible same-root plain triad/shell, test each candidate 7th, 9th, 11th, or 13th independently. If the extension itself is brief, off-beat, decorative, terminal, or unsupported by repetition/re-articulation, keep the plain triad/shell as primary and store the extension only as surface color.
decision_order: identify the most stable same-root plain triad or shell from bass plus structural tones
inspect each possible extension as a separate promotion candidate
require independent structural evidence for the extension itself
if evidence is insufficient, preserve the plain shell and do not let readability alone upgrade the label

negative_examples.context: F and A are structurally sustained; E appears only as a brief final decorative 16th note.
D triad is structural; E appears only as a short ornamental arpeggio step.

rules.interpretive_principle: Family readability alone is never enough for extension promotion.

rules.description: When a candidate root is suggested only by a very late or very early boundary-adjacent bass onset in humanized MIDI, do not let that bass override the current structural root unless the new bass is durationally meaningful or confirmed on the next strong pulse.

examples.input_context: A-C-Eb occupies most of the bar, B bass appears only at the very end
examples.reason: Boundary-adjacent B is treated as anticipation until structurally confirmed.

examples.input_context: New bass enters slightly early but continues through the next strong beat with supporting tones
examples.preferred_action: Promote new bass-supported chord at the confirmed boundary.

rules.description: A primary label may not ignore the sounding bass. If the upper structure suggests a different root, either write the slash/inversion explicitly or choose a functionally equivalent bass-aware label. For example, bass E with upper notes F-Ab-B-D may be labeled F°/E or E7(b9), but never bare F° as the selected label.

examples.input_pitch_set: E bass with upper notes F-Ab-B-D
examples.reason: The actual sounding bass must remain visible in the selected label.

rules.description: A brief bass note inside an otherwise stable chord should first be tested as melodic bass motion, passing bass, or line-cliché movement before being promoted to a new root. However, not every bass movement is melodic. If the new bass has meaningful duration and the upper structure also reorganizes around it, prefer a real chord split over forcing a single stable chord label.

examples.input_context: G/B sonority remains stable while bass briefly passes through F before continuing
examples.reason: A fleeting bass passing tone without matching structural takeover does not justify a new chord root.

examples.input_context: Bass moves from E to Bb and the upper notes reorganize from E-G#-B-D-F toward Bb with C-E-G before resolving to Am11
examples.preferred_action: Split into two structural chords instead of treating Bb as passing bass
examples.reason: New bass plus upper-structure reorganization indicates a genuine within-bar harmonic change.

rules.description: A changing bass note must not be interpreted in isolation. When bass motion occurs, determine whether it functions as melodic bass motion or as the root/bass of a new harmony by examining the simultaneous upper structure. If the upper harmony largely preserves the identity of the previous chord, interpret the bass motion first as passing bass, melodic bass line, line cliché, or connective low-note motion within the current harmony. If the bass changes together with a meaningful reorganization of the upper structure, and the new sonority is better explained as a distinct harmony than as a continuation of the previous one, promote it to a new chord candidate. Do not treat a bass change alone as sufficient evidence of harmonic change.

Upper-structure reorganization must not be inferred from a single decorative tone, brief non-chord tone, arpeggiated subdivision, or ornamental anticipation alone. Treat the upper harmony as genuinely reorganized only when the structurally relevant tones above the bass shift in a way that changes the harmonic identity more convincingly than a continuation of the previous chord.

Do not judge upper-structure reorganization by note count alone. A changed upper sonority should be treated as a true harmonic change signal only if it is musically interpretable as a coherent local harmony, rather than as melody notes, decorative fills, or arpeggiated continuation over the same bass.

examples.input_context: C major accompaniment remains over the same bass while upper notes move as a melodic line
examples.preferred_action: Keep the structural chord stable and treat the moving upper notes as melody or surface color
examples.reason: Upper-note motion alone does not create a new harmonic center when the local sonority is not independently chord-like.

rules.description: Treat upper-structure reorganization as strong evidence of a new local harmony when three conditions align: the bass changes, the upper sonority is itself harmonically interpretable, and the new sonority has meaningful relation to the surrounding progression or resolution. When these conditions are met, prefer explicit local chord segmentation over preserving the previous chord by inertia.

examples.input_context: Bass changes while the upper structure also forms a newly interpretable sonority that connects naturally with the previous and following chords
examples.preferred_action: Recognize a genuine local harmonic change
examples.reason: Bass motion plus interpretable upper re-centering plus progression logic together outweigh a surface-melody reading.

examples.input_context: Bass stays fixed while upper notes move melodically and do not form a convincing independent harmony
examples.preferred_action: Do not treat the passage as upper-structure reorganization strong enough to create a new chord
examples.reason: Without bass support and harmonic interpretability, the passage behaves more like melodic decoration than harmonic reset.

rules.description: In humanized MIDI, allow a within-bar chord split when the bass changes in a durationally meaningful way and the upper structure also re-centers. The analyzer should not preserve one label across the whole window merely to avoid over-segmentation.

examples.input_context: First half behaves like E7(b9), then Bb bass enters with upper C-E-G and resolves to Am11
examples.reason: Bass change plus upper-structure reset and semitone bass resolution justify separate local chords.

rules.description: When the upper structure forms a fully diminished seventh built a semitone above the bass-root candidate, strongly test the corresponding dominant7(b9) interpretation before emitting a bare diminished label that hides the bass.

examples.reason: The upper diminished set is real, but the final label must remain bass-aware and functionally useful.

rules.description: If the current slice contains only a shell or partial voicing, use surrounding bars/beats, bass continuity, guide-tone motion, and resolution tendency to recover the most likely functional label. Store the confidence penalty separately from the chosen label.

Before finalizing a functional label, compare it against the actually sounding structural tones. A functional label may infer omitted tones, but it should not erase strong observed tones that materially favor another local sonority unless emitted as an alternate.

If neither the major nor minor 3rd above the actual bass is structurally present, keep chord quality undecided at first. Before settling on m7, m7b5, diminished-fragment, or same-root tension labels, test whether the sonority is better captured by (1) a sus4 reading, especially when the perfect 4th above bass is structural, or (2) a literal slash-chord reading built from a coherent upper triad/shell over the bass.

Even if a same-root tension spelling is theoretically readable, do not promote to add9, 11, or other tension-bearing same-root labels when a literal slash-chord reading more directly matches the sounding upper structure and bass. Readability alone does not justify replacing a clear slash identity with a same-root omitted-3rd tension label.

meter_verification.description: Rules for verifying and potentially overriding the MIDI metadata time signature by analyzing actual rhythmic patterns in the performed note data. This is critical because many DAWs and notation programs export non-4/4 meters as 4/4 by default, and blindly trusting wrong metadata leads to incorrect harmonic segmentation.

rules.description: MIDI time-signature metadata is a hint, not ground truth. Always verify against actual onset patterns before using it for harmonic window segmentation.

Build an onset-position histogram modulo candidate cycle lengths. For each candidate meter (2/4, 3/4, 4/4, 6/8, 9/8, 12/8, 5/4, 7/8), map all note onsets to positions within one cycle and measure how well the velocity/density accent pattern matches the expected metric hierarchy.

Bass onsets are the strongest indicator of metric downbeats. Identify the periodic cycle of bass-note onsets and compare to candidate meters. In 6/8, bass typically occurs on the dotted-quarter pulse (every 3 eighth-notes). In 4/4, bass typically occurs on beats 1 and 3 (every 2 quarter-notes = 4 eighth-notes).

Higher velocity on certain positions indicates metric accent. Aggregate velocities at each position in the candidate cycle. Strong velocity peaks matching the candidate's primary_accent_positions increase confidence in that meter.
More simultaneous note onsets at a position suggest a structural downbeat. Chord attacks (multiple notes at once) tend to fall on strong beats.

Examine inter-onset intervals (IOIs). If the dominant IOI is a triplet subdivision of the quarter-note (tpqn/3 or tpqn*2/3), compound meter is likely. If the dominant IOI is a binary subdivision (tpqn/2 or tpqn), simple meter is likely.

examples.scenario: tpqn=480, dominant IOI=240 (eighth note in simple time) or 160 (triplet)

examples.interpretation: eighth-note grid, could be simple or compound depending on accent pattern
cycle of 720 ticks = dotted quarter = strong evidence for 6/8

rules.description: Distinguishing 6/8 from 3/4 when both have a 6-eighth-note cycle: 6/8 groups as 3+3 (two dotted-quarter beats, internal subdivision in threes), 3/4 groups as 2+2+2 (three quarter-note beats, internal subdivision in twos). Check whether inner subdivisions cluster at positions 0,2,4 (3/4 feel) or 0,3 (6/8 feel). Also check harmonic rhythm: if chords change at dotted-quarter intervals, 6/8 is more likely; if they change at quarter-note intervals, 3/4.

When the pattern-derived meter has significantly higher confidence than the MIDI metadata meter, override the metadata for all subsequent analysis steps. Log the override as a warning in the output report. If confidence is close (within 15%), report both interpretations and mark the analysis as meter-ambiguous.

When compound meter is confirmed (6/8, 9/8, 12/8), redefine analysis windows: the 'beat' unit becomes the dotted quarter-note (3 eighth-notes), the 'bar' becomes the full compound cycle, and 'half-bar' splits at the compound beat boundary (e.g. in 6/8, half-bar = 3 eighth-notes = one dotted quarter).

6/8.note: One bar of 6/8 = 6 eighth notes = 3 quarter notes worth of ticks. Primary beats fall at 0 and tpqn*3/2 (i.e. every dotted quarter).

anti_inertia_enforcement.description: Prevents the analyzer from freezing onto the first plausible chord inside a bar and ignoring later bass-supported harmonic arrivals.
rules: Treat the first within-bar label as provisional until later metrically meaningful bass arrivals have been audited.
A new bass plus shell reformation outranks comfort-driven preference for one neat bar-level label.
The question is not “can the old label somehow survive?” but “does the post-change region form its own better-supported local harmony from the new bass?”
If a later sonority is only validated locally, keep that validation local; do not smear it backward across earlier beats.

execution_guardrails.description: Forces a last-pass verification stage so provisional upper-structure recognition cannot silently collapse into premature simplified output labels.
rules: Separate observation, candidate generation, and final emission. A shape recognized in the upper structure may enter the candidate list without being licensed as the final label.
If a carried bass is still sounding, treat slash-preserving labels as the default branch until a genuine bass refresh is confirmed.
Do not replace a bass-preserving slash label with a cleaner unslashed label merely because the upper notes spell a familiar triad or seventh chord.
When in doubt between a simpler root-position label and a bass-preserving slash label, prefer the slash label unless a new bass attack clearly resets the harmonic floor.
Final-label readability is subordinate to sounding-bass truth. Canonical naming convenience must never override carried-bass preservation.
If the analyzer previously noted possible bass carry-over, that uncertainty must be revisited explicitly in the pre-output pass and may not be silently discarded.
In harmony-analysis mode, the pre-output pass must verify that no missing 3rd, 7th, or tension was silently supplied by melody evidence when selecting the accompaniment label.

If a clear upper triad/shell over bass survives to the pre-output pass, do not rewrite it into a same-root omitted-3rd tension label unless that rewrite is explicitly better supported than the literal slash reading.

avoid_tones.reason: minor ninth clash against major third on strong beat
clashes against major third unless treated as sus

final_label_policy.description: Do not use a catch-all dominant label in place of explicit dominant tension labeling. When structurally supported dominant tensions are present, append the actually supported tensions explicitly to the dominant label, such as 7(b9), 7(#9), 7(b13), 7(#11), 7(b9,b13), or other supported combinations. Do not promote brief ornamental, passing, anticipatory, or weakly supported tones into final tension text.

avoid_tones.reason: do not sustain major 3rd against 4th unless resolving

voicing_templates.name: explicit_tensioned_dominant_compact
voicing_templates.description: Compact voicing family for dominant chords whose supported tensions must be labeled explicitly rather than merged into a generic dominant bucket.

inversion_rules.notes: Use root position most often for strong arrival; first inversion allowed for smoother bass line.

generation_bias.first_inversion_common_use: When continuing a descending stepwise bass line through the tonic. Also common when tonic is not a structural arrival but a passing chord.

inversion_rules.notes: First and second inversion may be used to connect bass smoothly into dominant.

generation_bias.note: Predominant chords (ii, IV) are the most flexible for inversion since they serve a connective function. First and second inversions are common to create smooth approach to dominant.

inversion_rules.notes: Third in bass can strongly pull to tonic; fifth in bass is weak unless special line-cliche context.

generation_bias.note: Dominant chords gain enormous resolution energy from inversions. The leading tone or b7 in bass creates the strongest pull to tonic.

progression_resolution_rules.description: Minor-key iiø–V–i motion where the dominant should be labeled by its explicitly supported tensions rather than by a generic catch-all dominant label.

General secondary-dominant logic. If a dominant-quality sonority resolves convincingly to a local target chord, prefer the functional label V7/x over a literal enharmonic spelling. Use only when dominant evidence clearly exceeds borrowed/modal/color alternatives.
guardrails: Do not force V7/x if resolution is weak, delayed, stylistically coloristic, or better explained by modal interchange / passing color.

progression_resolution_rules.description: When a dominant resolves convincingly to vi, prefer V7/vi analysis even if MIDI pitch-classes look like an enharmonic flat-key dominant.
Suspended dominants may delay the third but should still be evaluated by their eventual dominant resolution behavior.
Borrowed iv in major or parallel-minor inflection should be available as a non-diatonic color option, especially in ballad, cinematic, and pop-jazz contexts.
bVII-family harmony may function as borrowed color, backdoor dominant, or modal-rock color; do not reduce every case to secondary-dominant logic.
Passing diminished chords often act as connective voice-leading devices rather than full functional dominants.
Allow tritone substitutions as a separate non-diatonic family when dominant-style resolution is present but root motion reflects substitute behavior.
When harmony moves in parallel color blocks without clear tonicization, preserve a non-functional/color interpretation instead of forcing dominant analysis.

rules.description: Top note may double melody when accompaniment is supportive and texture is sparse or medium.
Avoid top note creating harsh minor ninth clash against melody on strong beats.
If melody note is a passing tone / neighbor / suspension, accompaniment top note should prefer stable chord tone.
Prefer gentle contrary or oblique motion against melody in lyrical accompaniment.
If the melody note is a stable chord tone on a strong beat, prefer accompaniment voicings whose top note either matches the melody or stays safely below it without creating a clash.
On strong beats, accompaniment should favor root, 3rd, 7th, or clearly sanctioned style-dependent tensions rather than ambiguous chromatic color under the melody.
If melody already states the 3rd or 7th clearly, accompaniment should not heavily duplicate that same tone in the upper structure unless the texture specifically demands reinforcement.
A passing or neighbor melody note should not by itself trigger a drastic voicing rebuild or upper-structure relabeling. Preserve harmonic stability unless wider evidence confirms a new structure.

Melody-support rules operate after or alongside chord analysis for accompaniment design and evaluation. They must not be used to back-fill missing accompaniment chord tones into the primary analysis label.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: In arpeggio context, a brief E over D-F-A should not automatically force structural Dm9.

preferred_output.reason: Surface color note present, but structural label should remain stable in simple accompaniment arpeggio unless E is metrically or durationally emphasized.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: Same pitch collection may admit multiple labels. Prefer the more complete/contextually stable label.

candidate_chords.label: Fmaj9/A
reasons: Slash chord over A is common and readable
Phrase context may favor F-centered interpretation

candidate_chords.label: Am7(b13)
reasons: A-C-E-G present and F can function as b13
Less preferred as primary label if surrounding harmony suggests F-major center

candidate_interpretation_examples.selected_label_policy: Return both, choose one with higher context-weighted score.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: After a clearly established A#7/Bb7-like dominant sonority, a following D#m7 target should remain primary even if MIDI contains an F natural that could reflect enharmonic spelling ambiguity or a chromatic approach to F#.

preferred_output.reason: Functional context from the previous dominant and the presence of the D#m7 skeleton outweigh a single ambiguous MIDI pitch.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: Raw MIDI pitch-classes may resemble Bb7, but in F# major context the musically preferable functional label can be A#7 = V7/vi if the chord resolves to D#m7.

preferred_output.reason: Functional respelling is preferred over literal pitch-set naming when secondary-dominant resolution is clear.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: In a major-key ballad, an Fm or Fm6 moving to C may be better understood as borrowed iv color rather than any secondary-dominant chain.

preferred_output.reason: Plagal-color pull and parallel-minor borrowing explain the sonority more musically than dominant reinterpretation.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: A bVII or bVII7 resolving to I may function as borrowed/backdoor/modal color; do not force secondary-dominant labeling unless dominant evidence is clearly stronger.

preferred_output.reason: The move is better captured as borrowed/backdoor color than as a generic secondary dominant.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: When MIDI pitch-classes suggest Bb7 but context resolves strongly to D#m7 in F# major, respell functionally as A#7 and emit roman numeral V7/vi.

preferred_output.reason: Functional respelling plus roman numeral gives the musically useful interpretation.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: A strong maj7 with only a later brief off-beat 9 should remain Dmaj7, not Dmaj9.

preferred_output.reason: The maj7 independently qualifies for promotion, but the 9 does not.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: In a humanized non-quantized MIDI performance, a note from the next harmony that enters slightly early should not automatically force a relabel of the current bar.

preferred_output.reason: The late boundary note lacks enough metrical and durational control to take over the current bar.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: If the upper structure resembles a diminished chord but the actual bass is different, the selected label must keep the bass visible.

preferred_output.reason: Upper-structure recognition does not license removal of the actual bass from the final label.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: A brief bass passing tone inside a stable slash chord should not automatically create a new harmonic label.

preferred_output.reason: The stable G/B skeleton and duration occupancy outweigh the fleeting bass motion.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: A realistic performance voicing may omit several core tones while still clearly functioning as E7 from bass and context.

preferred_output.reason: Bass plus guide-tone identity and resolution behavior recover the dominant function even though the local slice is sparse.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: Even a very sparse slice such as bass E plus upper B can still be interpreted contextually, but with reduced confidence.

preferred_output.reason: Insufficient local completeness alone should not force rejection; keep confidence lower and rely on context.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: If the sounding structural tones already strongly identify a bass-aware dominant-b9 sonority, do not jump to an unrelated prettier functional label.

preferred_output.reason: The observed pitch-set already contains enough evidence for the bass-aware dominant-b9 reading.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: When a humanized bar contains two clearly supported local sonorities, do not merge them into one broad dominant label; keep the explicitly supported tensions of each local sonority.

preferred_output.reason: The first sonority already specifically supports E7(b9), and the later Bb bass with C-E-G forms a new local dominant sonority. Keep both local harmonies instead of collapsing the passage into one broad dominant name.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: Moving upper notes should not be promoted to a new chord unless the new upper sonority is itself chord-like and supported by bass/progression context.

preferred_output.surface_or_transition_tag: upper notes treated as melody over stable harmony
preferred_output.reason: Upper-note motion alone is not enough; no new harmonically interpretable local sonority with new bass/progression support is established.

candidate_interpretation_examples.wrong: D7/C automatically because C can be heard as the 7th of D
candidate_interpretation_examples.correct: D/C by default; promote to D7/C only if the C bass is structurally confirmed as the functional 7th.

candidate_interpretation_examples.reason: Perfect 4th above bass is present, 3rd is absent, and the next segment reveals the 3rd as sus resolution.

candidate_interpretation_examples.description: A later within-bar bass change can create a new valid dominant/inversion label, but that later label must remain local and must not overwrite the earlier beats.

preferred_output.forbidden_primary_label: single A7/C# label for the entire measure
preferred_output.reason: The A7/C# interpretation becomes valid only after the beat-3 bass change to C#, so a mandatory within-bar split is required.

analysis_pipeline.goal: Analyze MIDI using rule DB and return chord identification, inversion analysis, melody/top-note fit, playability, voice-leading quality, and rule compliance.
steps: Parse MIDI note events, tempo, time signature, tracks.
After parsing MIDI events, do not assume the metadata time signature is correct. Perform onset-pattern meter verification before splitting into analysis windows.

Meter verification procedure: (a) Collect all note-onset ticks. (b) Compute inter-onset intervals (IOIs) and find the dominant subdivision unit (typically the shortest frequently recurring IOI, e.g. eighth-note level). (c) Build a beat-position histogram by mapping onsets to positions within candidate grouping cycles (2-group for 2/4 or 6/8-as-2, 3-group for 3/4 or 9/8-as-3, 4-group for 4/4, 6-group for 6/8, etc.). (d) Evaluate accent strength at each histogram position using velocity, bass onsets, and note density. (e) Compare the accent profile against known meter templates and select the best-fit meter.

Meter template matching heuristics: 6/8 signature = strong accent every 3 eighth-notes with two-group hypermeter (onset peaks at positions 0 and 3 in a 6-slot cycle, bass typically on 0); 3/4 signature = strong accent every 3 quarter-notes (onset peaks at 0, may have secondary accent at 1 or 2); 4/4 signature = strong accent every 4 quarter-notes (peaks at 0 and 2); 12/8 or compound quadruple = strong every 3 eighth-notes with four-group hypermeter. If the best-fit meter differs from the MIDI metadata, override the metadata and log the discrepancy as a warning in the output.

When meter is determined to be compound (6/8, 9/8, 12/8), adjust all subsequent analysis windows to use dotted-quarter-note or dotted-beat boundaries instead of quarter-note boundaries. Beat strength mapping must reflect compound meter hierarchy: in 6/8 the dotted-quarter (3 eighth-notes) is the primary beat, not the quarter-note.
Split into analysis windows (bar, half-bar, beat, or event-based).
Estimate whether the MIDI is tightly quantized or humanized; if humanized, apply boundary-timing tolerance before declaring chord changes.
Merge or soften micro-overlaps around beat/bar boundaries so slight anticipations and delayed releases are first tested as expressive timing rather than immediate harmonic replacement.
Detect texture mode for each region before final segmentation decisions: block chord / homophonic, arpeggio / broken-chord, melodic-accompanimental, pedal-bass, or mixed.
If arpeggio / broken-chord texture is confirmed, separate sounding bass from harmonic bass before promoting any within-pattern low note to a new harmonic split.
In arpeggio-aware mode, do not finalize a new slash chord from a weak-position or terminal bass note unless an additional re-anchoring cue confirms that a genuinely new local harmony has formed.

Separate upper-structure recognition from final selected-label output: first detect possible diminished, tension-heavy, or quartal upper clusters, then choose a bass-aware slash or functionally reinterpreted label.
Before declaring a new root, test whether newly appearing bass notes are better explained as melodic bass motion, passing tones, line cliché, or arpeggio traversal within the current harmony.

If a new bass persists and the upper structure also re-centers, split the window into separate local harmonic events even inside a single bar; do not let anti-oversegmentation rules erase a real chord change.
When a dominant sonority is detected, prefer the most specific explicitly supported label (for example 7(b9)) and do not retreat to a generic catch-all dominant label.
Measure duration occupancy for bass notes and chord skeleton tones so long-held notes count as stronger structural evidence than brief notes.

In harmony-analysis mode, derive accompaniment chord labels from accompaniment and bass evidence only. Melody notes may be stored separately as role/context information, but they must not back-fill missing 3rds, 7ths, or tensions into the accompaniment label.
Estimate melody track or melody note if melody track is provided.
Classify the active melody note before voicing generation: chord tone, structural tension, suspension, passing tone, neighbor tone, appoggiatura, or avoid-tone candidate.
Generate multiple accompaniment voicing candidates per chord instead of a single default closed stack.

Score voicing candidates by melody support, non-collision with melody, duplication economy, common-tone retention, voice-leading smoothness, inversion quality, playability, and style fit before selecting the output voicing.
If the melody already states an important function tone, prefer support voicings that complement rather than redundantly thicken that same tone.
Apply explicit penalties for repeated adjacent root-3-5-7 close-position outputs unless an educational or deliberately block-chord mode was requested.
Estimate bass, top note, and left/right hand distribution.
Generate chord candidates per window.

When a coherent upper triad or shell appears over a different bass, rank the literal slash candidate before same-root omitted-3rd tension rewrites such as preferring G/A before Am11(no3), unless stronger evidence proves the same-root identity is more faithful.

Before accepting a single harmony for a full bar, run a mandatory split audit on every metrically meaningful new-bass event. If two or more split indicators are satisfied, reopen local analysis from the new bass and emit separate segments unless an explicit rejection reason is documented.
Score candidates using structural weighting, chord completeness, bass fit, context, melody relation, and rule DB.
After collecting notes for a window, separate literal sounding pitch-set evidence from contextual functional inference; do not skip the literal evidence check.
Detect whether the local slice is a complete chord, shell voicing, rootless voicing, upper-structure fragment, or incomplete performance fragment before final labeling.

If the local slice lacks both major and minor 3rds above the actual bass, keep chord quality undecided and test sus4/slash explanations before defaulting to m7, m7b5, or other diatonically convenient quality names.
Before finalizing the selected label, run an observed-pitch conflict check so functional reinterpretation does not erase clearly sounding structural tones.

Run a mandatory final-label preflight gate before emitting the selected label: verify actual sounding bass, verify whether a new bass attack truly occurred, verify whether any slash/inversion information is still required, and block readability-driven simplification if it would erase carried bass reality.
If a candidate was generated from upper-structure recognition alone, require explicit bass-aware confirmation before it can win final selection.
When useful, emit three layers separately: literal sounding set, upper-structure/partial-voicing interpretation, and final bass-aware selected label.
Select primary chord plus alternate candidates.
Evaluate voicing, inversion, top note, voice leading, tension validity, avoid tones, and playability.
Return overall report with bar/segment diagnostics and suggestions.
Estimate key center and local tonicization candidates before final functional labeling.

For each winning chord candidate, derive roman numeral, scale-degree root, and non-diatonic family (diatonic, borrowed, secondary dominant, tritone-sub, passing diminished, chromatic-planing, pedal/sus, color-chord).
Compare non-diatonic interpretations broadly; do not collapse every outside chord into secondary dominant if borrowed/color explanations fit better.
Apply enharmonic ambiguity handling: compare pitch-classes first, then determine final spellings from key center, functional resolution, and readability.
If melody-aware voicing mode is requested, rank voicing templates by melody protection, style fit, playability, and voice-leading quality.

scoring_model.description: Suggested weighted score components for each candidate and final report.

components.name: bass_carry_over_preservation
components.description: Rewards candidates that preserve still-sounding bass reality instead of prematurely relabeling to a cleaner root-position chord.

components.name: anti_premature_simplification
components.description: Penalizes candidates that erase slash information or imply an unsupported bass reset for readability.

notes: Hard-constraint violations should reject a candidate or apply a near-fatal penalty.
Soft constraints should rank otherwise valid candidates.
Structural label stability should favor musically readable labels over overfitted pitch-set labels.
When a dominant-resolution path is strongly supported, reduce penalty for a single ambiguous chromatic pitch if the destination chord skeleton is otherwise clear.
Enharmonic spelling confidence should be tracked separately from harmonic-fit confidence.
