If melody-aware voicing selection is enabled, preferred voicing templates should be ranked by melody support, playability, and style fit.
Non-diatonic harmony should be evaluated across borrowed, secondary-dominant, tritone-sub, passing-diminished, planing, pedal/sus, and color-chord families before final labeling.

In humanized performance MIDI, timing-boundary stability should reward analyses that resist unnecessary relabeling from slight anticipations, overlaps, or delayed releases until the new harmony is structurally confirmed.
Duration occupancy should reward analyses supported by long-held bass notes and sustained chord skeleton tones, especially in non-quantized legato MIDI.
Bass-motion coherence should reward analyses that correctly distinguish true root changes from melodic bass movement, line clichés, and passing bass notes.
Upper-structure recognition must be scored separately from final selected-label correctness so diminished or tension-heavy clusters do not erase the sounding bass in the final label.
Incomplete voicings should usually incur a confidence penalty rather than a hard rejection; contextual recovery and local resolution evidence may restore the winning label.
Observed-pitch-set integrity must be checked separately from contextual prettiness so the analyzer does not invent unsupported chord tones or ignore clearly sounding structural tones.

When a literal upper structure and a functional bass-aware interpretation differ, preserve both internally and score final-label correctness by whether the sounding bass remains visible or functionally accounted for.
Melody support should be decomposed internally into melody-role recognition, accompaniment support quality, and top-voice non-collision rather than treated as a single vague preference.
If the melody already states a structural function tone, duplication economy should reward support voicings that complement the melody instead of thickening the same degree by default.
Naive repeated close-position root stacks should incur a separate penalty because they often indicate chord-tone dumping rather than contextual piano voicing.
Voicing generation should compare multiple candidates per chord event; selecting directly from a single template lookup is insufficient for melody-aware accompaniment.
Generation-mode scoring should explicitly separate bass-candidate quality from upper-voice optimization; otherwise root-position defaults may win too often by inertia.
Guide-tone connection quality deserves its own tracked score because a voicing can be harmonically correct yet still sound stiff if 3rds and 7ths do not connect musically.
Pattern-aware inversion fit should boost good ii-V-I, chromatic approach, descending-third, and circle-motion solutions, but must remain subordinate to melody protection and hard constraints.
Repeated close-position root stacks across multiple chord events should be penalized even if each individual stack is technically valid.

mode.description: When set to 'generation' or 'analysis_and_generation', the voicing_generation_rules are activated and the engine will produce MIDI voicings, not just labels.

chord_progression_input.description: Chord symbols for generation mode. Each entry is a chord symbol string (e.g., 'DMaj7', 'C#m7b5') with optional bar position and duration.

bass_line_priority.description: Overall bass line strategy hint for generation.

inversion_freedom.description: How freely the generator may use inversions. 'strict_root_only' disables inversions entirely. 'aggressive' and 'rootless_allowed' permit very free voice leading.

developer_notes: Start with a rule-based analyzer first; add ML/AI later as a contextual assistant, not as the only judge.
Treat melody as top-priority when melody track exists.
Return primary and alternate chord labels rather than pretending ambiguity does not exist.
Separate structural harmony and surface-note interpretation in both internals and output.
For accompaniment analysis, avoid overreacting to every passing/arpeggiated color tone.
This JSON is intended as a starting spec; weights should be tuned empirically on real MIDI examples.
Add explicit enharmonic-respelling logic because MIDI pitch-classes do not preserve true spellings.
Treat secondary dominants functionally first, then assign the most readable enharmonic chord label for the current key context.
Expand this spec toward melody-aware voicing recommendation, not only chord identification.
Maintain a gold test set of real MIDI excerpts and regress every rule change against it.
Do not let the system develop a reflex where every non-diatonic sonority becomes a secondary dominant; maintain broad comparison across borrowed/modal/color families.
Emit roman numeral, scale-degree root, tonicization target, and non-diatonic family separately from literal chord name.
When user asks for a fresh outside chord, the engine should search across multiple non-diatonic strategies rather than defaulting to V7/x.
Evaluate structural extensions per tone, not as a bundle: a structural maj7 does not automatically imply structural 9, 11, or 13.
Distinguish humanized performance timing from structural harmonic timing; slight early/late notes near boundaries should be tested as anticipation or overlap before relabeling harmony.
Maintain test cases with non-quantized human MIDI so the analyzer does not confuse expressive timing with real mid-bar chord changes.

Maintain test cases where note length itself encodes sustain; the analyzer should treat long-duration occupancy as structural evidence rather than flattening everything into simultaneous pitch-class sets.
Do not let brief melodic bass motion or arpeggiated traversal create a new primary chord unless duration and supporting tones confirm a true harmonic takeover.
Upper-structure analysis is not the same as final labeling: if the bass is E, a detected F-diminished upper structure cannot be emitted as bare F°.
Do not convert missing core tones into a hard ban. Real performance MIDI often uses shell voicings, omitted fifths, and sparse right-hand fragments that still imply clear harmony from context.
But contextual recovery of omitted tones is not permission to ignore the actual sounding pitch-set; check for strong observed-tone conflicts before finalizing a prettier functional label.
Maintain test cases for sparse but valid dominant shells (for example bass E with G# only, or bass E with B only) so the analyzer learns confidence reduction instead of false rejection.
Maintain explicit regression tests for upper diminished structures over a different bass (for example F° over E) so the analyzer outputs a bass-aware label such as E7(b9) or F°/E, never bare F°.
Maintain regression tests for bars containing two real harmonic arrivals under humanized timing so the analyzer does not flatten mid-bar chord changes into a single merged label.

If a later sonority in the same bar gains its own bass support and upper-structure identity, preserve that second harmonic center explicitly instead of treating it as mere overlap from the previous chord.

Do not emit a generic catch-all dominant label as a final chord label. For dominant sonorities, write only the structurally supported tensions explicitly, such as 7(b9), 7(#9), 7(b13), 7(#11), or supported combinations.

Upper-structure reorganization should be granted only when the new upper sonority is genuinely harmonically interpretable; mere melodic motion above a stable bass must not be mistaken for a new chord.
When bass change, interpretable upper-sonority change, and surrounding progression logic align, prefer an explicit local chord change even inside one bar.
Practical labeling is a readability preference, not a license to erase heard harmonic identity.

Many DAWs (Logic Pro, Ableton, FL Studio, etc.) export MIDI with default 4/4 time signature even when the actual project uses a different meter. The meter_verification system was added to catch this common problem before it corrupts all downstream harmonic segmentation.

Meter detection should run BEFORE harmonic window splitting. Getting the meter wrong means every segment boundary is wrong, which cascades into wrong pitch collections per segment, wrong bass identification, and ultimately wrong chord labels. This is a foundational step.
This schema now distinguishes analysis guidance from accompaniment-voicing generation guidance more explicitly.
A compliant MIDI generator should not emit accompaniment directly from chord labels without melody-role classification and candidate comparison.

The original JSON was designed as an analysis/evaluation engine. When used for GENERATION (composing, arranging), the system must also have rules that PRESCRIBE inversions and voice leading, not just SCORE them after the fact. Without generation rules, the safest default (root position everything) always wins because it never triggers penalties.
Bass line generation should be treated as a FIRST-CLASS compositional decision, not an afterthought. Lock in the bass contour before filling inner voices.
The generation pipeline produces voicings in a specific order: bass → guide tones → inner voices → melody check → playability check. This order is critical because each layer constrains the next.

Style profiles for inversion frequency are targets, not hard limits. A jazz piece that happens to have root-friendly voice leading may end up with more root position than the target — that's fine as long as the generator actually evaluated alternatives.

When the user specifies a chord progression for generation, the generator must STILL run analysis on the output to verify that the generated voicings would pass the analysis-mode rules. Generation and analysis are complementary, not separate.
Do not auto-upgrade an upper-structure triad over a non-chord bass into a functional seventh slash chord. D/C is the default; D7/C must be earned.
Sus4 recognition is not merely a preference. If the 4th above bass is structural and the 3rd is absent, enter a sus4-only evaluation branch before any alternate-root minor-shell recovery.
Do not summarize a bar with a single representative chord if a later beat introduces a genuinely new bass-supported harmony. Report the split explicitly.
A later dominant/inversion reinterpretation is segment-local evidence, not permission to relabel the whole bar retroactively.

Repeated error pattern to guard against: selecting one early chord and then spending the rest of the bar trying to preserve it. The engine must be willing to discard the first guess when a later bass-supported shell forms.
Regression-test every update against bars where beat 3 or beat 4 introduces a real new harmony. Passing those tests matters more than preserving pretty one-chord summaries.
When refusing to split, log the exact anti-split reason. “Still probably the same chord” is not an acceptable reason.

Execution guardrail principle: most repeat errors happen at final emission, not initial observation. Therefore the engine must treat pre-output relabeling as a danger zone and re-audit bass carry-over, slash necessity, and whether an apparent upper-structure chord ever received a true bass reset.
Bass-first analysis remains the default, but arpeggio texture must distinguish sounding bass from harmonic bass so terminal broken-chord tones do not create false local splits.
v2.08: added strict analysis-vs-generation separation, literal slash-before-no3 tension preference, and no-3rd quality-undecided/sus4-first safeguards.

enharmonic_interpretation_rules.description: MIDI loses true enharmonic spelling, so analysis should compare pitch-class input first and assign final note names from harmonic context, key center, and resolution direction.

rules.description: Treat pitches like E# and F, B and Cb, A# and Bb, C# and Db as the same pitch-class at input time. Do not trust literal note spelling from MIDI as final harmonic spelling.

Choose the final enharmonic spelling from surrounding key center, structural chord function, and expected resolution. Prefer musically readable spellings such as A#7 for V7/vi in F# major even if raw MIDI pitch-classes resemble Bb7.
A single chromatic pitch that can be explained as enharmonic respelling, passing tone, or approach tone should not automatically overturn a structurally well-supported chord candidate.

melody_voicing_rules.description: Rules for selecting or generating piano voicings when a melody note is given. These rules extend harmony analysis into melody-constrained voicing recommendation.

rules.description: If melody note is a chord tone or stable tension, prefer voicings that keep the melody as the top note or place the accompaniment top note safely below it.
If melody is a passing, neighbor, or suspension tone, accompaniment should prioritize stable guide tones and avoid competing chromatic color on top.
For piano accompaniment, choose left-hand foundation by style and density: root+5 or root+10 for ballad/pop, shell/rootless variants for jazz where context is clear.
Prefer common-tone retention and stepwise top-note motion between adjacent voicings. Resolve dominant 7ths and explicitly named dominant tensions with clear direction when possible.
Permit or restrict upper tensions according to style. Ballad favors supportive color; jazz/modern_jazz may use denser explicit-tension or rootless shapes.

Before voicing selection, classify the active melody note as chord_tone_primary, structural_tension, suspension, passing_tone, neighbor_tone, appoggiatura, or avoid_tone_candidate. Voicing generation must use this classification before choosing any template or inversion.
Allow different voicings for the same chord label when the melody note changes role, register, accent, or sustain profile. Never assume one fixed voicing shape per chord symbol.
If the melody already states the 3rd, 7th, or a sanctioned structural tension, lower duplication priority for that tone inside the accompaniment and favor complementary shell/support tones instead.

If the melody note is non-structural passing, neighbor, or lightly accented appoggiatura material, keep the accompaniment harmonically stable unless additional evidence clearly supports a chord change or revoicing.

The top accompaniment voice must not sit above or psychoacoustically overshadow the melody unless deliberately arranged as parallel support. Prefer accompaniment top notes below the melody when the melody is the intended lead.

Evaluate inversion by melody-bass interaction as well as bass smoothness. Favor inversions that avoid both melody and bass making large simultaneous leaps and that encourage contrary or oblique motion when musically appropriate.

Repeated root-3-5-7 close-position output across adjacent events should be penalized unless explicitly requested, because it usually indicates chord-tone dumping instead of melody-aware piano voicing.

roman_numeral_interpretation_rules.description: Convert absolute chord interpretations into roman-numeral / degree labels from estimated key center and local tonicization. Keep this separate from literal chord spelling. Non-diatonic harmony should be classified across multiple families before defaulting to secondary dominant.
workflow: Estimate global key center, then allow local tonicization or temporary local center when contextual evidence is strong.
Map chord root to scale degree relative to estimated key center or local tonicized center.
Determine whether the chord is diatonic, borrowed/modal-interchange, secondary dominant, tritone substitution, chromatic passing, suspended dominant, pedal-harmony, or other non-diatonic color.
Do not derive roman numerals until after bass-aware selected labeling is settled; upper-structure labels and literal fragments should not be mistaken for the final functional root.
Emit literal chord label and roman numeral separately.
If multiple readings remain plausible, keep primary and alternate roman numerals rather than collapsing everything into a dominant interpretation.

non_diatonic_families.when_to_prefer: Dominant-quality chord resolves convincingly to a local target chord whose arrival is supported by guide-tone motion and contextual cadence.
Borrowed color from parallel mode is more convincing than dominant resolution, especially in pop/ballad/cinematic contexts.
Dominant substitute root lies tritone from expected dominant and preserves dominant-style resolution behavior.
Chord mainly smooths bass/top-line motion and does not establish stable tonicization.
Diminished structure serves connective voice leading more than functional dominant purpose.
Pedal or suspension explains the sonority more musically than forcing a new functional root.
Style and texture suggest color sonority rather than classical-functional resolution.

voicing_generation_policy.description: Explicit procedure for generating accompaniment voicings from chord-plus-melody context rather than from chord label alone.
steps: Identify the active melody note or melody fragment for the local analysis window.
Classify the melody role as structural chord tone, sanctioned structural tension, suspension, passing tone, neighbor tone, appoggiatura, or avoid-tone candidate.
Generate multiple voicing candidates per chord label across template families, inversions, registers, and duplication patterns instead of using a single default stack.

For each candidate, evaluate melody support, top-voice non-collision, duplication economy, common-tone retention, inner-voice motion, inversion suitability, bass coherence, playability, and style fit.
Reject or heavily penalize candidates that expose strong melody collisions, repeated naive close-position root stacks, or unnecessary duplication of melody-declared function tones.
Select the highest-scoring candidate and retain alternates for diagnostics.
candidate_generation_guidelines: Per chord event, generate at least 6 candidate voicings when melody is present.
Vary inversion, register span, left-hand foundation, right-hand guide-tone placement, and whether melody is doubled, under-supported, or left fully exposed.
Do not restrict a chord to one house voicing; same chord labels may map to different voicings depending on melody and context.
integration_notes: Bass-line design is a first-class decision. Candidate voicings should be grouped by bass choice before inner voices are optimized.
Guide tones are locked before color-tone decoration whenever possible.
Melody-aware scoring may override a generic progression-pattern inversion recommendation when the recommendation would create melody collision, unnecessary duplication, or top-voice clutter.
Pattern-based inversion strategies are preferences; they must yield when hard constraints, phrase structure, or melody support argue otherwise.

Melody-aware acceptance of omitted accompaniment chord tones belongs to generation/voicing-validation mode and must not retroactively rewrite the accompaniment chord label produced by harmony-analysis mode.

generation_extension_meta.purpose: Adds generation-mode rules for bass line selection, inversion choice, and voice leading when the engine is used to COMPOSE or ARRANGE rather than ANALYZE. Without this module, the engine defaults to root position for all chords because the analysis-only rules contain no prescriptive generation logic.

voicing_generation_rules.description: Rules for GENERATING voicings and selecting inversions when composing or arranging piano accompaniment. These rules are active only in generation/composition mode, not in analysis mode. They ensure smooth voice leading, musically appropriate bass lines, and style-aware inversion choices.

bass_line_generation.description: Bass note selection must NOT default to root position. The generator MUST evaluate all inversion options for each chord and select the bass note that produces the best bass line contour.

hard_rules.description: For every chord in the progression, the generator MUST compute at least root position, first inversion (3rd in bass), and second inversion (5th in bass) candidates. For seventh chords, also evaluate third inversion (7th in bass). Do NOT default to root position without comparison.

If three or more consecutive chords would all use root position AND the bass intervals between them are all larger than a perfect 4th (5 semitones), at least one chord MUST use an inversion to smooth the bass line.

When two adjacent chord roots are exactly 1 semitone apart (e.g., D to C#, C to B), ALWAYS keep both in root position to preserve the chromatic bass motion. An inversion would destroy the strongest possible bass voice leading.

preference_rules.description: Prefer bass lines that move by step (1-2 semitones) or small skip (3-4 semitones). Penalize large leaps unless they serve a structural purpose.
When an inversion creates a chromatic bass line (consecutive half-steps across 3+ chords), give a significant bonus. Chromatic bass lines are among the strongest motivations for inversions.
In ballad and pop contexts, gently descending bass lines are generally preferred over ascending or jagged contours.
Avoid monotonous bass contour. After 4+ consecutive descending steps, allow ascent. After 3+ static (common tone) bass notes, introduce motion.
At phrase boundaries, cadence points, section starts, and tonic arrivals, prefer root position. Inversions are for connecting structural pillars, not for the pillars themselves.

Dominant 7th chords resolving to tonic strongly benefit from inversions that place tendency tones in the bass: 3rd in bass (= leading tone) resolving up to tonic root, or b7 in bass resolving down to tonic 3rd.

inversion_selection_by_progression.description: Specific inversion recommendations for common chord progression patterns. When the current chord sequence matches a known pattern, these recommendations supplement the generic bass line preferences.

recommended_bass_strategies.bass_motion_description: 3rd of ii → b7 of V → root of I, creating stepwise descent
recommended_bass_strategies.note: Best voice leading. Bass walks down through guide tones.

recommended_bass_strategies.bass_motion_description: root of ii → 3rd of V (leading tone) → root of I
recommended_bass_strategies.note: Leading tone in bass of V pulls strongly up to I.
Acceptable but misses voice leading opportunities.
Leading tone (3rd of V7) in bass resolving up to minor tonic root.

patterns.description: When adjacent roots are a semitone apart, root position is already optimal.
examples: DMaj7 → C#m7b5 : D → C# = semitone descent, keep root
Cmaj7 → Bm7 : C → B = semitone descent, keep root
Fm7 → E7 : F → E = semitone descent, keep root
patterns.note: Treat semitone-related roots as a strong default root-position opportunity, not an absolute law.

patterns.description: When roots descend by 3rds (e.g., I-vi-IV-ii), alternating root and 1st inversion creates a smooth stepwise bass.
In a circle-of-4ths progression (common in jazz), the bass can be smoothed by alternating root position with 2nd inversion, creating a pattern of descending steps instead of ascending 4ths.
examples: E7(root=E) → A7(2nd inv, E in bass): common tone bass, then move
Alternative: E7(b7=D bass) → A7(root=A): stepwise D→A descent through D

voice_leading_generation.description: Rules for generating inner voice movement between chords. Runs after bass selection.

rules.description: The 3rd and 7th (guide tones) of each chord should connect to the 3rd or 7th of the next chord by step (semitone or whole tone) or common tone. Lock guide tones in FIRST, before other voices.

common_connections.context: V→I when 3rd of V = 7th of I

rules.description: If adjacent chords share a pitch class, at least one voice must hold that common tone. Do not move all voices simultaneously.
When bass descends, prefer upper voices to ascend or hold. When bass ascends, prefer upper voices to descend or hold. Contrary motion creates voice independence.
Each inner voice should move by the smallest possible interval to reach a chord tone of the next chord. Leaps in inner voices are discouraged unless necessary.
Avoid parallel perfect 5ths and octaves between any two voices in consecutive chords.
Tendency tones must resolve in their expected direction unless a deliberate deceptive effect is intended.
Each voice should stay within a reasonable register band across the progression. Avoid abrupt register shifts in inner voices. Bass may shift registers at phrase boundaries.

generation_pipeline.description: Step-by-step procedure for generating a complete piano voicing for a chord progression.
steps: Parse chord progression into individual chord symbols with root, quality, and any specified tensions.
Identify functional relationships: ii-V pairs, tonic arrivals, chromatic approaches, circle-of-4ths chains, deceptive resolutions.
Identify phrase boundaries and structural pillars.
FOR EACH CHORD: identify the active melody note or melody fragment before candidate generation, and classify its harmonic role.
FOR EACH CHORD: compute all viable bass note candidates (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th in bass).
Score each bass candidate using bass_line_generation.preference_rules, considering previous and next bass notes.
Check against inversion_selection_by_progression patterns — if matched, apply pattern-specific recommendations.
Select highest-scoring bass note. If tie, prefer the option closest to the previous bass note.
Generate multiple chord-plus-melody voicing candidates per bass choice across register, duplication pattern, and template family.
Lock in guide tones (3rd and 7th) connecting to adjacent chords by step or common tone.
Fill remaining inner voices using voice_leading_generation rules.
Reduce unnecessary duplication when the melody already states a structural function tone such as 3rd, 7th, or a sanctioned structural tension.
Apply melody_voicing_rules during candidate generation and scoring, not only as a final afterthought.
Check playability_constraints from main rule DB.
Check hard_constraints from main rule DB.
If any hard constraint violated, try next-best bass candidate and re-run from step 8.
Reject or strongly penalize repeated naive close-position root stacks unless style, register, and melody context explicitly justify them.
Retain at least one alternate candidate and diagnostic score trace for debugging why the selected voicing won.
Output final voicing as MIDI note events with explicit bass, inner voices, and top note.

style_inversion_profiles.description: Style-specific defaults for inversion frequency and behavior.

profiles.note: Jazz piano often uses rootless voicings; when bass is present, inversions are very common for smooth lines.
Ballads use inversions primarily for descending bass lines and chromatic approaches.
Pop uses fewer inversions but still benefits from them in pre-chorus builds and transitional passages.
Modern jazz piano frequently omits the root entirely (rootless voicings) or uses 3rd/7th in bass as default.

melody_generation_bridge.description: Generation mode must read melody context as early input rather than only validating after voicing construction.
requirements: Melody-role classification happens before final inversion and duplication choices.
Same chord labels may map to different voicings when melody role, register, accent, or sustain changes.
Accompaniment top voice must not overshadow the melody unless a deliberate parallel-support texture is requested.

texture_sensitive_segmentation_rules.description: Texture-aware exceptions that modify how bass motion is interpreted without weakening the existing bass-first guardrails in normal accompaniment.

modes.default_bass_policy: Strongly respect bass change and slash-preserve meaningful bass events.
Do not equate every newly lowest note with a new harmonic bass. First test whether the note is part of the active harmony's broken-chord pattern.

db_storage_schema.description: Storage schema for saving analysis results into reusable DB rows for retrieval and melody-aware voicing generation.

composer_friendly_summary.harmonic_event: 한 화성 구간이 실제로 어떻게 들렸는지를 저장하는 기본 단위
composer_friendly_summary.pattern_fragment: 나중에 다른 코드 진행에도 다시 쓸 수 있는 반주 패턴 조각
composer_friendly_summary.retrieval_tags: 비슷한 상황의 패턴을 검색하기 위한 꼬리표

storage_principles: 코드명 하나만 저장하지 말고 베이스, 탑노트, 텐션, 리듬, 보이스리딩 정보를 함께 저장한다.
절대 음고 정보와 전조해도 유지되는 정규화 정보를 둘 다 저장한다.
harmonic_event는 분석용 기본 단위, pattern_fragment는 재배치 및 생성용 단위로 구분한다.
retrieval_tags는 melody-aware retrieval, style-aware retrieval, texture filtering에 활용한다.

harmonic_event.description: One locally valid harmonic segment or chord event.

pattern_fragment.description: Reusable short accompaniment pattern unit for rearrangement/generation.

retrieval_tags.description: Tag axes for similarity search and retrieval filtering.

song_form_analysis.description: Song-form-aware section inference for MIDI accompaniment data. This module does not assume fixed bar counts such as 4-bar intros or mandatory intros. It estimates likely section roles from multiple musical cues, stores uncertainty explicitly, and links harmonic events to section context for later retrieval/generation.
design_principles: Do not classify sections by fixed length rules such as 'first 4 bars = intro' or 'pre-chorus must be 4 bars'.
Treat section labeling as probabilistic inference, not deterministic truth.
Allow songs with no intro, long intros, truncated forms, repeated choruses, or ambiguous hybrid sections.
Store a primary section label, secondary descriptive tags, and confidence rather than forcing a single absolute answer.
Allow unknown, ambiguous, or hybrid section labels when evidence is weak or mixed.
Use multiple cues together: repetition, melodic entry, density, register lift, cadence strength, bass-pattern reset, rhythmic change, and phrase recurrence.
Section analysis should help retrieval and generation, not overwrite local harmonic analysis.
section_inference_pipeline: detect candidate boundary points from cadence, texture change, melodic-entry change, repeating pattern resets, and density/register shifts
group local harmonic events into provisional section spans
score each provisional span against multiple section-role hypotheses
select primary label only if score separation is meaningful
if score separation is weak, emit unknown or hybrid with secondary tags and confidence
map every harmonic_event to its parent section and local position inside that section

multi_cue_scoring_axes.melodic_entry_strength: Whether a clear lead melody begins or meaningfully changes in role
multi_cue_scoring_axes.pattern_repetition: Whether a harmonic/rhythmic idea recurs later in the piece
multi_cue_scoring_axes.energy_level: Low / medium / high perceived energy from density, accenting, and register spread
multi_cue_scoring_axes.register_trend: Low-centered, mid-centered, expanding upward, or released downward
multi_cue_scoring_axes.cadence_strength: Weak / medium / strong closure at the section end
multi_cue_scoring_axes.bass_reset_strength: Whether bass contour suggests fresh section start or phrase restart
multi_cue_scoring_axes.hook_recurrence: Whether material resembles a repeated refrain/hook
multi_cue_scoring_axes.entry_vs_transition_behavior: Whether the span behaves more like a true section or a connector

classification_policy.if_margin_below_threshold: emit unknown or hybrid instead of forcing a section label

retrieval_usage_examples: Prefer verse-like low-to-mid energy patterns when generating a verse accompaniment.
Prefer build-tagged patterns near a prechorus ending.
Prefer chorus-like high-energy or hook-recurrence patterns when the target section needs a refrain feel.
Avoid retrieving dense chorus voicings for a sparse intro-like direct-entry section unless explicitly requested.
